data.title: Duolingo Refocuses On Long Term Growth As AI Spend And Buybacks Rise
data.description:
• Duolingo (NasdaqGS:DUOL) is shifting its focus toward long term user growth, higher AI investment and expanding beyond languages into subjects like music and math.
  • Management has lowered guidance for near term bookings and profitability as part of this reset, contributing to recent share price volatility.
  • The company has also announced a $400m share repurchase program alongside these changes.

For investors watching NasdaqGS:DUOL, the timing of this pivot comes after a tough period for the share price. Duolingo last closed at $101.54, with the stock down 13.5% over the past week, 15.6% over the past month and 42.5% year to date. Over the past year, the share price has fallen 62.8%, and over three years the return sits at 16.4%.

The combination of heavier AI spending, a push into new learning categories and a $400m buyback creates a different mix of growth and risk compared with Duolingo’s previous strategy. As these choices flow through to user trends and financial results, investors may track how engagement in new areas like music and math develops alongside the core language app.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Duolingo by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Duolingo.

📰 Beyond the headline: 2 risks and 3 things going right for Duolingo that every investor should see.

For Duolingo, this reset is happening from a position of current financial strength rather than distress. In 2025, sales were US$1.04b and net income was US$414.07m, with fourth quarter sales of US$282.87m and net income of US$41.95m. Management is now choosing to lean into heavier AI and marketing spend, accept slower bookings and margin pressure in 2026, and push harder on user growth and new subjects like music and math. That trade off can change how you think about the business model. The focus moves from near term profitability toward long term engagement and the size of the user base, in a market where large tech players such as Duolingo’s education peers on mobile, as well as AI tools from companies like Google and OpenAI, compete for learning time.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Duolingo Narrative

  • The emphasis on AI powered tutoring and new subjects such as Music and Math aligns with the narrative that an expanded product set and higher engagement could support long term growth in users and revenue.
  • Guidance for slower bookings growth and narrower margins challenges the view that monetization and profitability will progress smoothly, and highlights that near term earnings may be more volatile than the earlier narrative suggested.
  • The US$400m buyback, shelf registration for 2,331,377 shares related to employee plans, and recent share price volatility introduce capital allocation and dilution factors that are not fully reflected in the original storyline about user and product expansion.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Duolingo to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Analysts have highlighted guidance for slower bookings growth and weaker adjusted EBITDA margins in 2026, which could keep earnings under pressure while spending on AI and marketing rises.
  • ⚠️ Concerns about AI based competition in education apps and an investigation by Faruqi & Faruqi, LLP into potential investor claims add legal and competitive uncertainty on top of recent analyst downgrades.
  • 🎁 Earnings grew very strongly year over year in 2025, and Duolingo has recently passed US$1b in annual sales, which supports the view that the platform can generate meaningful profit at scale.
  • 🎁 Simply Wall St data indicates earnings growth over the past year is very large and the shares are trading at a discount to one estimate of fair value, which some investors may see as a potential opportunity if the long term user growth plan works as intended.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, it will be important to watch whether Duolingo’s heavier AI and marketing spend translates into a clear re acceleration in daily and monthly active users, both in core language courses and newer subjects like music and math. Investors may also track how bookings growth, adjusted EBITDA margins and net income trend relative to the 2026 guidance that triggered the recent pullback. The pace of share repurchases under the US$400m program, any use of the shelf registration tied to employee stock plans, and further commentary from analysts on competitive threats from AI tools will help shape how the market views the risk reward trade off.

data.title: Elia Group (ENXTBR:ELI) Earnings Growth Of 32.1% Tests Premium P/E Narrative
data.description:
Advertisement

Earnings snapshot and context

Elia Group (ENXTBR:ELI) has wrapped up FY 2025 with second half revenue of €2.3b and basic EPS of €2.63, while trailing twelve month figures show revenue of €4.4b and EPS of €5.51, anchoring the latest earnings release. The company has reported an increase in revenue from €2.0b in the second half of 2024 to €2.3b in the second half of 2025, with net income excluding extra items rising from €239.7m to €287.0m over the same period. This gives investors a clearer view of how the earnings power has been building into a wider margin profile and frames how they might interpret this set of results.

See our full analysis for Elia Group.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to weigh them against the widely held stories about Elia Group, checking where the latest margins and growth trends align with the narrative and where they start to challenge it.

See what the community is saying about Elia Group

Margins hold up as revenue base expands

  • On a trailing basis, Elia Group generated €4.4b in revenue and €556.6m in net income excluding extra items, which works out to a 12.5% net margin compared with 11.2% a year earlier.
  • Analysts' consensus view links this higher 12.5% net margin and 32.1% earnings growth over the last year to ongoing grid investment. It also notes that forecasts build in mid teens revenue and earnings growth, which will need to be supported by efficient execution on the large CapEx program and evolving regulatory frameworks.
    • Consensus narrative expects revenue to grow about 13.9% per year and earnings about 14.7% per year. Margin assumptions for the future are a bit lower than today, and this sits alongside the recent margin lift in the reported numbers.
    • The same narrative highlights large offshore and interconnector projects and updated cost plus models as drivers for revenue and earnings. The current 12.5% margin and €556.6m of trailing net income are key reference points for judging how that thesis plays out over time.

Premium P/E and DCF gap stand out

  • Elia Group trades on a P/E of 25.8x at a share price of €131.90, above the European Electric Utilities industry average of 15.3x and peer average of 18.3x, while the DCF fair value in the data is €55.04.
  • Critics highlight that this higher 25.8x multiple and the gap between the €131.90 share price and €55.04 DCF fair value sit alongside balance sheet flags. They therefore question how much of the strong trailing 32.1% earnings growth is already embedded in the valuation.
    • The same analysis points out that debt coverage by operating cash flow is described as weak and that shareholders were substantially diluted over the past year. This is linked to the richer valuation as a potential risk factor.
    • While earnings growth has been solid on a trailing basis and margins have risen to 12.5%, skeptics argue that paying a P/E well above industry and peer levels leaves less room for error if growth or regulatory outcomes differ from expectations.
Over 30% earnings growth and a premium P/E have investors split on whether Elia's price fully reflects the risks and rewards, and you can see how both sides frame that debate in the 📊 Read the what the Community is saying about Elia Group..

Half year mix and funding trade offs

  • Within FY 2025, first half revenue of €2.17b and net income of €269.6m combined with second half revenue of €2.27b and net income of €287.0m, while over the last year shareholders also experienced significant dilution linked to funding the CapEx pipeline.
  • Consensus narrative notes that a record investment program is supported by a strengthened capital structure. At the same time, the data showing shareholder dilution and weak debt coverage by operating cash flow illustrate the trade off between financing growth and protecting existing holders.
    • The trailing 12 month revenue of €4.4b and net income of €556.6m provide the earnings base against which the recent equity raise and additional long term debt facilities are assessed by investors who focus on return on that new capital.
    • Expectations for mid teens annual revenue and earnings growth are being weighed against the reality that shares outstanding are expected to keep rising and that operating cash flow currently does not comfortably cover debt.

Next Steps

With opinions split across these results, do you feel the balance of risks and rewards justifies the current setup, or not quite yet? Take a moment to weigh the numbers yourself and see where you land, then use our breakdown of 2 key rewards and 2 important warning signs to round out your view.

See What Else Is Out There

data.title: ServiceNow AI Push Targets Regulated Growth In Government And Healthcare
data.description:
• ServiceNow (NYSE:NOW) has launched new AI driven platforms, Autonomous Workforce and EmployeeWorks, targeting government and healthcare clients.
  • The company is integrating capabilities from its Moveworks acquisition and new partnership with Autonomize AI into these offerings.
  • Forrester recently recognized ServiceNow as a leader in public sector cloud solutions, underscoring its push into regulated markets.

ServiceNow enters this phase of product expansion with a mixed performance profile. The share price stands at $120.38, with the stock up 10.1% over the past week and 9.7% over the past month, while return over the past year shows a 30.6% decline and year to date an 18.4% decline. Over a longer horizon, the 3 year return is 40.5% and the 5 year return is 19.7%.

For investors watching NYSE:NOW, the expansion into public sector and healthcare AI may be relevant if you are focused on companies building broader platforms for large, regulated clients. The new AI offerings and recent recognition in public sector cloud could influence how the market evaluates ServiceNow's role in enterprise automation, particularly for longer term theses around mission critical workflows.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for ServiceNow by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on ServiceNow.

We've flagged 0 risks for ServiceNow. See which could impact your investment.

This news points to ServiceNow leaning harder into regulated, high-complexity workflows where AI and compliance matter just as much as automation speed. EmployeeWorks and Autonomous Workforce are built specifically for public sector environments such as Government Community Cloud and National Security Cloud, which often have long procurement cycles, high switching costs, and strict governance. For investors, that focus can be important because it ties AI directly to mission-critical work such as IT service, employee support and cross agency coordination, rather than treating AI as an add on feature.

The Autonomize AI partnership extends that logic into healthcare payers, where claims processing, fraud detection and utilization management are tightly regulated and operationally intensive. Here, ServiceNow is positioning its AI Platform as the orchestration layer for domain specific agents from partners like Autonomize AI. Combined with identity security integrations from 1Kosmos and application security ties with Contrast Security, the company is building out an ecosystem around its AI Control Tower rather than going it alone.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The ServiceNow Narrative

  • The push into EmployeeWorks, Autonomous Workforce and healthcare payer workflows aligns with the narrative that AI focused products and acquisitions such as Moveworks can expand ServiceNow’s addressable market and support longer term revenue growth.
  • Heavier exposure to U.S. federal agencies and healthcare payers echoes one of the flagged risks, because contract specific budgets, policy changes or slower adoption could affect how quickly these AI offerings scale.
  • The newer telecom roaming collaboration with NTT DOCOMO and StarHub, and the Autonomize AI partnership around payer workflows, go beyond the narrative’s emphasis on public sector and CRM expansion and may not yet be fully reflected in longer term expectations.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for ServiceNow to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Higher reliance on government and healthcare contracts can expose ServiceNow to policy changes, budget constraints and lengthy sales cycles that may slow adoption of AI driven workflows.
  • ⚠️ Integrating multiple acquired and partner technologies, such as Moveworks and Autonomize AI, into a single AI Platform introduces execution and product complexity that competitors like Salesforce and Microsoft have also faced.
  • 🎁 Embedding AI specialists and conversational interfaces directly into core workflows can deepen ServiceNow’s role as a workflow hub, which may support customer stickiness versus peers such as other workflow and CRM software providers.
  • 🎁 Recognition by Forrester as a leader in public sector industry cloud, alongside expanding alliances in telecom and healthcare, positions ServiceNow as a key platform provider for organizations looking to apply AI at scale to operational processes.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, investors may want to watch how quickly public sector and healthcare clients adopt EmployeeWorks and Autonomous Workforce once the first AI specialist moves from controlled availability to full release. Adoption metrics such as the number of agencies using AI specialists for Level 1 IT support, early use cases from payers working with Autonomize AI, and any updates on FedRAMP or similar authorizations will be worth tracking. It is also useful to watch how often ServiceNow highlights AI driven deals or renewals compared with more traditional workflow wins, as that can give a feel for how central these new offerings are becoming in customer conversations.

Nordic Halibut FY 2025 earnings snapshot

Nordic Halibut (OB:NOHAL) has just wrapped up FY 2025 with fourth quarter revenue of NOK45.5 million and EPS of NOK0.54, while trailing 12 month figures show revenue of NOK145.5 million and a small loss equating to EPS of NOK0.19. The company has seen quarterly revenue move from NOK31.7 million in Q4 2024 to NOK45.5 million in Q4 2025, with EPS shifting from a loss of NOK0.62 to a profit of NOK0.54 over the same period. Trailing revenue rose from NOK90.6 million to NOK145.5 million as the trailing loss narrowed from NOK2.55 to NOK0.19 per share. Overall, the latest quarter points to firmer margins against a still loss making 12 month backdrop, which is what investors will be weighing as they look for earnings quality to catch up with the growth story.

  • What stands out for a bullish view is how the TTM revenue of NOK145.5 million sits alongside a much smaller loss of NOK10.0 million, which supports the idea that higher sales are starting to have more impact on the bottom line than in the prior TTM period.

Premium P/S of 7.9x versus peers

Short cash runway and huge DCF gap

    • At the same time, the flag that cash runway is under one year, combined with ongoing TTM losses of NOK10.0 million, underlines that any bullish view is heavily dependent on the company securing enough capital to reach the growth that underpins that DCF figure.

The Excess Returns model looks at how much profit a bank can earn on its equity above the return that shareholders require, and then capitalizes that difference to estimate what the shares might be worth today.

For Atlantic Union Bankshares, the starting point is a Book Value of US$35.31 per share and an Average Return on Equity of 10.61%. What counts as a normal or fair P/E often reflects the trade off between growth expectations and risk. Higher expected earnings growth or lower perceived risk can support a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk tends to line up with a lower P/E.

data.title: AppFolio (APPF) Is Up 7.5% After Raising 2026 Revenue And Margin Targets - Has The Bull Case Changed?
data.description:
• AppFolio recently outlined that revenue growth is outpacing customer additions as users adopt more paid services, supporting higher free-cash-flow margins and a broader mix of income streams, including services sold directly to residents on its platform.
  • Management’s guidance for 2026, including revenue of about US$1.10 billion and a larger adjusted operating margin near 26.5%, highlights how operating leverage and deeper monetization per unit are reshaping the company’s profit profile.
  • We’ll now examine how this stronger monetization and margin guidance may influence AppFolio’s existing investment narrative built around AI and high-margin services.

AppFolio Investment Narrative Recap

To own AppFolio, you need to believe its AI-enabled platform and high-margin services can keep lifting revenue per unit while defending pricing power in a crowded property software market. The latest update that revenue growth is outpacing customer additions supports this monetization story, but does not remove the key near term risk that rising competition in AI-powered tools and resident services could pressure both margins and customer growth if differentiation slips.

The most relevant recent announcement is AppFolio’s 2026 outlook for about US$1.10 billion in revenue and an adjusted operating margin near 26.5%. Coming after a year where sales rose but profit margins fell, this guidance matters for the catalyst around operating leverage and deeper monetization across payments, screening, and resident-facing services. It sets a clear marker that future progress will be judged on how efficiently AppFolio converts that higher revenue into durable earnings quality.

Exploring Other Perspectives

Before this news, the most optimistic analysts were already assuming roughly US$1.10 billion of revenue and about US$149 million of earnings by 2028, which is far more upbeat than the baseline view. In light of AppFolio’s stronger monetization and heavier reliance on resident services from Task 3, this new guidance could either support that bullish case or force a rethink, so it is worth weighing how your expectations compare with these very different scenarios.
